aiPersona:
You are an expert HVAC technician and contractor with 20 years of experience in residential and commercial heating, ventilation, and air conditioning systems. You speak the language of the trade naturally, referencing SEER2 ratings, AHRI matched systems, Manual J load calculations, and refrigerant handling procedures without hesitation.

You understand the difference between a package unit and a split system, know when a zoning system makes sense versus a single-stage setup, and can explain why a TXV is preferred over a fixed orifice in most applications. You are comfortable discussing tonnage sizing, static pressure, duct design, and airflow balancing.

When estimating jobs, you think in terms of equipment cost, materials (linesets, disconnects, pads, thermostats), labor hours based on difficulty and access, and code-required items like condensate traps, float switches, and permit fees. You always factor in refrigerant type — whether the system uses R-410A or the newer R-454B — and account for recovery and disposal when replacing older R-22 units.

You know that a straightforward change-out in a ground-floor closet is very different from a rooftop package unit swap or an attic air handler replacement with limited access. You price accordingly and flag access issues, crane requirements, or code upgrades proactively.

You are familiar with manufacturer warranty registration, AHRI matchup requirements, and the importance of documenting proper subcooling and superheat at startup. You recommend maintenance plans because you know they reduce callbacks and extend equipment life.

Your communication style is professional but approachable. You explain technical concepts in plain language when talking to homeowners, but you can go deep on specifications when talking to other techs or inspectors. You always prioritize safety, code compliance, and doing the job right the first time.

defaultLineItems.description: High-efficiency condenser unit, SEER2 rated, R-410A refrigerant
Variable-speed air handler or 80%+ AFUE gas furnace
AHRI-matched evaporator coil, cased, with TXV
Wi-Fi enabled programmable thermostat
Insulated copper lineset, 3/8 x 3/4 inch, 25ft standard

defaultLineItems.itemName: Condensate Drain Line and Safety Switch
defaultLineItems.description: PVC condensate drain, float switch, and condensate trap per code
Non-fused disconnect box with flexible conduit whip
Composite equipment pad for outdoor unit
R-410A refrigerant charge, system-specific amount
Full system installation — 2 technicians, standard difficulty
Charge verification, airflow testing, thermostat programming
City/county mechanical permit and inspection fee
Ultraviolet germicidal light installed in air handler
HVAC surge protection device for condenser
Modify existing ductwork to fit new equipment
High-efficiency condenser unit with R-410A
AHRI-matched evaporator coil with TXV
Insulated copper lineset, standard length
System charge, verified at startup
Non-fused disconnect and flexible conduit
AC condenser and coil swap — 2 techs
Permit and inspection fee
High-efficiency gas furnace, variable-speed blower
PVC venting for high-efficiency or B-vent for standard
Flexible gas connector, shutoff valve, drip leg
Condensate line and pump if needed for high-efficiency unit
Furnace swap — 2 techs, standard difficulty
Permit and inspection fee
Ductless heat pump condenser, inverter-driven compressor
Wall-mounted evaporator head with remote control
Pre-charged or field-charged lineset with control wiring

New 20A or 30A dedicated circuit from panel to disconnect
Mini-split install — 1-2 techs, wall penetration and mount
Permit and inspection
Full system inspection, coil cleaning, refrigerant check, electrical tightening
Furnace inspection, burner cleaning, safety checks, filter replacement
Standard pleated air filter, MERV 8-11
Chemical coil cleaning for heavy buildup

On-site diagnostic and troubleshooting, first 30 minutes
Technician labor beyond diagnostic, per hour
Replacement parts as identified during diagnosis
R-410A refrigerant per pound if low charge detected

terminologyGlossary.condenser: The outdoor unit that releases heat from the refrigerant to the outside air
terminologyGlossary.evaporator coil: The indoor coil that absorbs heat from the air inside the home
terminologyGlossary.air handler: The indoor unit containing the blower and evaporator coil
terminologyGlossary.SEER2: Seasonal Energy Efficiency Ratio (updated 2023 testing standard) — higher is more efficient
terminologyGlossary.AFUE: Annual Fuel Utilization Efficiency — percentage of fuel converted to heat in a furnace
terminologyGlossary.tonnage: Cooling capacity measurement — 1 ton equals 12,000 BTU per hour
terminologyGlossary.BTU: British Thermal Unit — basic measure of heating/cooling energy
terminologyGlossary.refrigerant: The chemical compound that absorbs and releases heat as it circulates through the system
terminologyGlossary.R-410A: Standard refrigerant for residential AC systems since 2010, replacing R-22
terminologyGlossary.R-22: Legacy refrigerant (Freon), phased out — no longer manufactured
terminologyGlossary.TXV: Thermostatic Expansion Valve — controls refrigerant flow into the evaporator
terminologyGlossary.subcooling: Temperature below the condensing point — used to verify proper refrigerant charge
terminologyGlossary.superheat: Temperature above the evaporating point — used to verify system performance
terminologyGlossary.static pressure: Air pressure in the duct system — measured in inches of water column (iwc)
terminologyGlossary.Manual J: Industry-standard calculation method for determining heating/cooling load of a building
terminologyGlossary.AHRI match: Equipment combination certified by AHRI to work together at rated efficiency
terminologyGlossary.lineset: Insulated copper tubing connecting the outdoor condenser to the indoor evaporator coil
terminologyGlossary.disconnect: Electrical safety switch mounted near the outdoor unit for service shutoff
terminologyGlossary.condensate: Water produced by the cooling process that must be properly drained
terminologyGlossary.variable speed: Blower or compressor that adjusts speed for comfort and efficiency rather than on/off cycling

seasonalTips.tip: Spring AC tune-up campaign — push preventive maintenance plans before summer heat arrives
Pre-summer rush — schedule system replacements now before emergency calls consume all capacity
Emergency repair calls peak — consider extending hours and adding helper techs to handle volume
Transition season — promote dual tune-ups (AC + heating) at a bundled discount
Furnace season starting — run heating tune-up and carbon monoxide safety campaigns
Slow season for installs — offer off-season pricing to keep crews busy through winter

followUpToneNotes: Reference seasonal urgency, energy savings, and home comfort. Mention efficiency ratings (SEER2, AFUE) and potential utility rebates. Emphasize warranty coverage and the cost of delaying replacement versus ongoing repair bills.
